question_variation: What is an artificial neural network?
context: An artificial neural network is a computational model inspired by biological neural systems. It consists of layers of connected units that transform input features into increasingly useful representations.
answer_hint: An artificial neural network is a layered model of interconnected units that learns patterns from data.

question_variation: What are the main components of a neural network?
context: A typical neural network contains an input layer, one or more hidden layers, an output layer, weights, biases, and activation functions.
answer_hint: The main components are layers, weights, biases, and activation functions.

question_variation: How are neural networks trained?
context: Neural networks are trained by comparing predictions with targets, computing a loss, and updating weights using gradient-based optimization and backpropagation.
answer_hint: Neural networks are trained by minimizing loss with backpropagation and gradient-based optimization.

question_variation: Why are activation functions used in neural networks?
context: Activation functions introduce nonlinearity. Without them, stacked layers would behave like a single linear transformation and the network would not model complex patterns well.
answer_hint: Activation functions add nonlinearity so the network can learn complex relationships.

question_variation: Why is TensorFlow associated with neural networks in the course?
context: The first week combines ANN concepts with TensorFlow because TensorFlow is a common framework for implementing, training, and evaluating neural networks.
answer_hint: TensorFlow is used as a practical framework for implementing and training neural networks.

question_variation: What is TensorFlow?
context: TensorFlow is an open-source machine learning framework used to build and train models such as neural networks. It provides tensor operations, automatic differentiation, and tools for deployment.
answer_hint: TensorFlow is a machine learning framework for building and training models.

question_variation: Why is TensorFlow useful in this course?
context: TensorFlow helps students move from theory to implementation by providing a practical way to build neural and transformer-based models.
answer_hint: TensorFlow is useful because it lets students implement course concepts in practice.

question_variation: What does automatic differentiation do in TensorFlow?
context: Automatic differentiation tracks operations and computes gradients automatically, which makes backpropagation practical for deep learning models.
answer_hint: Automatic differentiation computes gradients automatically for training.

question_variation: How does TensorFlow support model training?
context: TensorFlow supports model training through optimizers, loss functions, gradient computation, and high-level APIs such as Keras.
answer_hint: TensorFlow supports training with losses, optimizers, and high-level model APIs.

question_variation: What is a convolutional neural network?
context: A convolutional neural network is a deep learning model that uses convolutional filters to detect local patterns, especially in images.
answer_hint: A CNN is a neural network that uses convolutional filters to learn spatial patterns.

question_variation: Why are CNNs effective for image tasks?
context: CNNs exploit spatial structure by applying the same filters across an image. This allows them to detect edges, textures, and shapes efficiently.
answer_hint: CNNs are effective for images because they learn local spatial features efficiently.

question_variation: What does a convolution layer do?
context: A convolution layer applies learnable filters to an input and produces feature maps that highlight specific local patterns.
answer_hint: A convolution layer creates feature maps by applying learned filters.

question_variation: What is pooling in CNNs?
context: Pooling reduces the spatial size of feature maps while retaining the most important information. It is often used to reduce computation and improve robustness.
answer_hint: Pooling downsamples feature maps to reduce computation and keep key information.

question_variation: Why are CNNs included in an LLM course?
context: The course includes CNNs because it also covers image processing and multimodal topics, giving students a broader deep learning foundation before focusing on transformers and LLMs.
answer_hint: CNNs provide a deep learning foundation and support the image-processing side of the course.

question_variation: What does deep learning for image processing mean?
context: Deep learning for image processing refers to using neural networks, especially CNNs and transformer-based models, to analyze and understand visual data.
answer_hint: It means using deep models to analyze and interpret images.

question_variation: What kinds of tasks are included in image processing?
context: Image processing tasks include classification, detection, segmentation, enhancement, and recognition.
answer_hint: Common image-processing tasks include classification, detection, and recognition.

question_variation: Why is image processing part of this course?
context: The course explicitly mentions image processing and image-language applications, so students see how transformer methods extend beyond text.
answer_hint: Image processing is included because the course also covers transformer-based visual and multimodal applications.

question_variation: What is natural language processing?
context: Natural language processing is the field of enabling computers to analyze, represent, and generate human language.
answer_hint: NLP is the study of methods that allow computers to process and generate human language.

question_variation: What are the goals of NLP?
context: NLP aims to support tasks such as text classification, information extraction, translation, summarization, question answering, and dialogue.
answer_hint: NLP aims to help systems understand and generate language for practical tasks.

question_variation: How does traditional NLP differ from modern NLP?
context: Traditional NLP often relied on hand-crafted rules and features, while modern NLP uses learned representations and neural architectures such as transformers.
answer_hint: Traditional NLP relied more on rules and manual features, while modern NLP relies more on learned neural representations.

question_variation: When is NLP introduced in the course?
context: Introduction to Natural Language Processing is covered in Week 4, followed by deep learning for NLP in Week 5.
answer_hint: NLP is introduced in Week 4 and expanded with deep learning in Week 5.

question_variation: What is tokenization in LLMs?
context: Tokenization is the process of splitting text into smaller model-processable units called tokens. These units may be whole words, subwords, or symbols depending on the tokenizer.
answer_hint: Tokenization is the process of splitting text into model-processable units called tokens.

question_variation: What are tokens in large language models?
context: Tokens are the basic units of text that a language model processes. A token may represent a whole word, part of a word, punctuation, or another text fragment.
answer_hint: Tokens are the units of text that a language model processes.

question_variation: Is one word always one token?
context: No. In many tokenizers, one word is not always one token. Some words split into multiple subword tokens, while short common words may map to a single token.
answer_hint: No, one word is not always one token.

question_variation: Why is tokenization important?
context: Tokenization matters because it determines how text is represented before modeling. It affects vocabulary design, sequence length, memory usage, cost, and how efficiently the model can process language.
answer_hint: Tokenization matters because it affects sequence length, vocabulary design, and model efficiency.

question_variation: Why do LLMs often use subword tokenization?
context: Subword tokenization helps models handle rare or unseen words without requiring an enormous vocabulary. It balances vocabulary size and language coverage.
answer_hint: LLMs often use subword tokenization to balance vocabulary size and coverage.

question_variation: How is tokenization related to LLM cost?
context: Many LLM systems charge and scale computation by token count rather than word count. More tokens usually mean higher cost and a larger portion of the context window is used.
answer_hint: Tokenization affects LLM cost because usage and context limits are usually measured in tokens.

question_variation: What is the difference between tokenization and embeddings?
context: Tokenization breaks text into tokens, while embeddings map those tokens into dense numeric vectors that capture useful properties for modeling.
answer_hint: Tokenization creates tokens, while embeddings convert tokens into vectors.

question_variation: Why do tokens matter for context windows?
context: A model's context window is measured in tokens, not words. If a text tokenizes into many tokens, it uses more of the available context budget.
answer_hint: Tokens matter because context windows are measured in tokens rather than words.

question_variation: What does tokenization mean in language models?
context: Tokenization means segmenting text into tokens that the model can process internally.
answer_hint: Tokenization means splitting text into tokens for model processing.

question_variation: What exactly are tokens in LLMs?
context: In LLMs, tokens are the atomic text units the model consumes, such as subwords, words, or punctuation fragments.
answer_hint: Tokens are the text units that LLMs consume.

question_variation: Why do tokens matter for large language models?
context: Tokens matter because sequence limits, cost, and many model operations are based on token counts rather than word counts.
answer_hint: Tokens matter because LLM limits and costs are measured in tokens.

question_variation: What does deep learning for NLP mean?
context: Deep learning for NLP means using neural architectures to learn language representations directly from data rather than relying only on manually designed features.
answer_hint: It means applying neural models to language tasks.

question_variation: Why use deep learning in NLP?
context: Deep learning helps NLP systems learn distributed and contextual representations that can generalize across tasks and large datasets.
answer_hint: Deep learning is useful in NLP because it learns rich language representations from data.

question_variation: Why does the course move from NLP introduction to deep learning for NLP?
context: The course first introduces core NLP ideas, then moves to neural approaches so students understand both the task space and the modeling techniques.
answer_hint: The course first introduces NLP basics and then moves to neural methods for deeper modeling.

question_variation: What is a transformer?
context: A transformer is a sequence modeling architecture built around attention mechanisms rather than recurrence. It became the dominant architecture for modern NLP and many multimodal tasks.
answer_hint: A transformer is an attention-based sequence model widely used in modern NLP.

question_variation: Why are transformers associated with attention?
context: Attention is central to transformers because it lets tokens directly interact with one another to form contextual representations.
answer_hint: Transformers rely on attention to build contextual token representations.

question_variation: Why were transformers a major step beyond RNNs?
context: Transformers improved parallelization and made it easier to model long-range dependencies using attention rather than sequential recurrence.
answer_hint: Transformers improved efficiency and long-range modeling compared with RNN-based methods.

question_variation: What is the encoder-decoder idea in transformers?
context: In encoder-decoder transformers, the encoder builds contextual representations of the input, while the decoder generates the output sequence using those representations.
answer_hint: The encoder processes input context, and the decoder generates output from that context.

question_variation: When are transformers covered in the course?
context: Transformers are covered explicitly in Week 6 with BERT and NER, and they also appear later in efficient AI, multilingual NER, and LLM-related weeks.
answer_hint: Transformers are introduced in Week 6 and continue to appear in later course topics.

question_variation: What is attention in transformers?
context: Attention is a mechanism that lets a model assign varying importance to different tokens when building a representation for the current token.
answer_hint: Attention lets the model weigh which tokens are most relevant to each other.

question_variation: What is self-attention?
context: Self-attention means tokens in the same sequence attend to one another to build contextualized representations.
answer_hint: Self-attention allows tokens in one sequence to interact directly with one another.

question_variation: Why is attention important?
context: Attention is important because it helps models capture long-distance relationships and context-dependent meaning more effectively.
answer_hint: Attention is important because it helps the model capture relevant context and long-range dependencies.

question_variation: Why is multi-head attention used?
context: Multi-head attention lets the model attend to multiple types of relationships in parallel rather than relying on a single attention pattern.
answer_hint: Multi-head attention allows the model to capture different relationships at the same time.

question_variation: What is BERT?
context: BERT stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. It is a transformer encoder model trained to produce contextual language representations.
answer_hint: BERT is a bidirectional transformer encoder model for contextual language understanding.

question_variation: What does bidirectional mean in BERT?
context: Bidirectional means BERT uses both left and right context when forming a representation for a token, rather than processing text in only one direction.
answer_hint: BERT is bidirectional because it uses both left and right context.

question_variation: What is BERT used for?
context: BERT is widely used for classification, named entity recognition, extractive question answering, and other language understanding tasks.
answer_hint: BERT is commonly used for language understanding tasks such as classification and NER.

question_variation: What is the difference between BERT and GPT?
context: BERT is an encoder-focused model for language understanding, while GPT is decoder-oriented and optimized for text generation.
answer_hint: BERT is mainly for understanding, while GPT is mainly for generation.

question_variation: Why is BERT used for NER?
context: BERT is often used for NER because it provides strong contextual token representations, which help the model assign entity labels to tokens more accurately.
answer_hint: BERT is often used for NER because it provides strong contextual token representations.

question_variation: How is BERT used for NER?
context: In BERT-based NER, each token representation is fed into a prediction layer that assigns an entity tag such as person, organization, or location.
answer_hint: BERT is used for NER by predicting an entity label for each token from contextual representations.

question_variation: When is BERT covered in the course?
context: The syllabus places BERT in Week 6 together with transformers and NER.
answer_hint: BERT is covered in Week 6.

question_variation: What is named entity recognition?
context: Named entity recognition is the task of identifying and labeling spans of text such as person names, organizations, and locations.
answer_hint: NER is the task of identifying and labeling named entities in text.

question_variation: Why is NER a token-level task?
context: NER is typically framed at the token level because the model predicts labels for each token or token span based on its contextual role in a sentence.
answer_hint: NER is a token-level prediction task because labels are assigned to tokens or spans.

question_variation: Why are transformers useful for NER?
context: Transformers help NER because contextual representations make it easier to distinguish the role and meaning of tokens in context.
answer_hint: Transformers are useful for NER because they provide context-aware token representations.

question_variation: How is NER positioned in the course?
context: NER appears in Week 6 with BERT and transformers, and multilingual NER follows in Week 7.
answer_hint: NER is covered first with BERT in Week 6 and then in multilingual form in Week 7.

question_variation: What is multilingual NER?
context: Multilingual NER applies named entity recognition across more than one language, often using shared transformer representations.
answer_hint: Multilingual NER is NER performed across multiple languages.

question_variation: Why is multilingual NER challenging?
context: It is challenging because languages differ in grammar, scripts, morphology, and data availability, so cross-lingual generalization is difficult.
answer_hint: Multilingual NER is challenging because languages differ in structure and available data.

question_variation: Why are transformers useful for multilingual NER?
context: Transformers can share representations across languages and support transfer learning, which helps multilingual sequence labeling tasks.
answer_hint: Transformers help multilingual NER by supporting shared multilingual representations.

question_variation: When is multilingual NER covered?
context: The syllabus places Multilingual Named Entity Recognition with Transformers in Week 7.
answer_hint: Multilingual NER is covered in Week 7.

question_variation: What does making transformers efficient in production mean?
context: It means adapting transformer models to run with lower latency, lower memory usage, and lower deployment cost while preserving useful accuracy.
answer_hint: It means optimizing transformer models for faster and cheaper real-world deployment.

question_variation: How can transformers be made more efficient?
context: Common approaches include pruning, quantization, distillation, model compression, efficient architectures, and serving optimizations.
answer_hint: Transformers can be made more efficient using methods such as pruning, quantization, and distillation.

question_variation: Why does the course include efficient AI?
context: Efficient AI is included so students understand not only how to build models, but also how to deploy them in realistic production settings.
answer_hint: The course includes efficient AI to connect modeling with practical deployment concerns.

question_variation: What is a vector database?
context: A vector database stores embeddings and supports efficient similarity search over high-dimensional vector representations.
answer_hint: A vector database stores embeddings and supports efficient similarity search.

question_variation: Why are vector databases useful?
context: They allow systems to retrieve semantically similar documents or records quickly, which is essential in retrieval-augmented systems.
answer_hint: Vector databases are useful because they enable fast semantic retrieval.

question_variation: How is a vector database related to RAG?
context: In RAG systems, embeddings of documents are stored in a vector database so relevant context can be retrieved for a user query.
answer_hint: Vector databases support RAG by storing document embeddings for retrieval.

question_variation: When are vector databases covered in the course?
context: The weekly schedule places vector databases in Week 10.
answer_hint: Vector databases are covered in Week 10.

question_variation: Which week covers vector databases?
context: Vector Databases are listed in Week 10 of the weekly schedule.
answer_hint: Vector databases are covered in Week 10.

question_variation: What is RAG?
context: RAG stands for Retrieval Augmented Generation. It combines retrieval of external context with generation or response construction based on that context.
answer_hint: RAG combines retrieval of external knowledge with answer generation.

question_variation: How does RAG work?
context: A RAG system first retrieves relevant information, then uses that retrieved context to produce a grounded answer.
answer_hint: RAG works by retrieving relevant context first and then generating an answer from it.

question_variation: Why is RAG used?
context: RAG is used to improve factual grounding, reduce unsupported answers, and make responses dependent on an external knowledge source.
answer_hint: RAG is used to ground answers in retrieved evidence.

question_variation: What is the difference between RAG and fine-tuning?
context: RAG injects external knowledge at inference time through retrieval, while fine-tuning changes the model parameters using additional training data.
answer_hint: RAG adds external knowledge at inference time, while fine-tuning changes the model weights.

question_variation: Does the course include RAG?
context: Yes. The weekly schedule explicitly lists Retrieval Augmented Generation.
answer_hint: Yes, the course includes RAG.

question_variation: When is RAG covered in the course?
context: The weekly schedule lists Retrieval Augmented Generation in Week 11.
answer_hint: RAG is covered in Week 11.

question_variation: Which week covers RAG?
context: Retrieval Augmented Generation appears in Week 11 of the course schedule.
answer_hint: RAG is covered in Week 11.

question_variation: What is an LLM?
context: An LLM is a large language model trained on large-scale text corpora to model and generate language.
answer_hint: An LLM is a large language model trained on large text corpora.

question_variation: Why are they called large language models?
context: They are called large because they are trained on very large datasets and usually have a very large number of parameters.
answer_hint: They are called large language models because they are trained at large scale and usually have many parameters.

question_variation: What are LLMs used for?
context: LLMs are used for question answering, summarization, dialogue, text generation, classification, reasoning assistance, and other language tasks.
answer_hint: LLMs are used for tasks such as question answering, summarization, and text generation.

question_variation: How are LLMs trained?
context: LLMs are typically trained with large-scale self-supervised objectives, then adapted through instruction tuning, fine-tuning, or retrieval-based methods.
answer_hint: LLMs are trained with large-scale language modeling objectives and later adapted for specific uses.

question_variation: When are LLMs covered in the course?
context: The weekly detailed schedule places Large Language Models in Week 12.
answer_hint: LLMs are covered in Week 12.

question_variation: Why do tokens matter in LLMs?
context: LLMs process token sequences rather than raw words, so tokenization directly affects context length, cost, and representation quality.
answer_hint: Tokens matter because LLMs process sequences in tokens rather than raw words.

question_variation: What is fine-tuning in LLMs?
context: Fine-tuning is the process of adapting a pretrained model to a downstream task or domain using additional training data.
answer_hint: Fine-tuning adapts a pretrained model to a specific task or domain.

question_variation: Why is fine-tuning useful?
context: Fine-tuning helps align a model with domain-specific tasks or behavior when general pretraining alone is not enough.
answer_hint: Fine-tuning is useful for specializing a general model to a target task.

question_variation: How is fine-tuning different from RAG?
context: Fine-tuning updates model parameters, while RAG retrieves external context without changing the model weights at inference time.
answer_hint: Fine-tuning changes model weights, while RAG retrieves external context.

question_variation: Does the course include LLM fine-tuning?
context: Yes. LLM fine-tuning is explicitly listed in the course content.
answer_hint: Yes, the course includes LLM fine-tuning.

question_variation: What are MLLMs?
context: MLLMs are multimodal large language models that process and reason over multiple data types such as text and images.
answer_hint: MLLMs are multimodal large language models that combine language with other modalities such as images.

question_variation: Why are multimodal models important?
context: They allow a system to integrate information from text and vision or other modalities, which supports richer applications.
answer_hint: Multimodal models are important because they combine information from multiple input types.

question_variation: Does the course include MLLMs?
context: Yes. MLLMs are explicitly listed in the course content.
answer_hint: Yes, the course includes MLLMs.

question_variation: What is prompting in LLM systems?
context: Prompting is the practice of structuring instructions and context so a language model produces a desired response.
answer_hint: Prompting is the process of shaping a model's input to guide its output.

question_variation: Why is prompting important?
context: Prompting matters because the quality, clarity, and structure of the input can strongly affect model output.
answer_hint: Prompting is important because model behavior depends strongly on how the input is phrased.

question_variation: How does prompting relate to RAG?
context: In RAG systems, the retrieved context is inserted into the prompt so the model can answer using grounded evidence.
answer_hint: In RAG, prompting is used to present retrieved context to the model.

question_variation: What is the difference between tokenization and embeddings?
context: Tokenization breaks text into discrete units, while embeddings convert those units into dense vectors for model processing.
answer_hint: Tokenization creates tokens, while embeddings convert tokens into vectors.

question_variation: What is the difference between BERT and GPT?
context: BERT is an encoder-oriented model for understanding language, while GPT is decoder-oriented and optimized for generation.
answer_hint: BERT is mainly for understanding, while GPT is mainly for generation.

question_variation: What is the difference between RAG and fine-tuning?
context: RAG retrieves external knowledge at inference time, while fine-tuning changes model weights using additional training data.
answer_hint: RAG adds external knowledge at inference time, while fine-tuning updates model parameters.

question_variation: What is the difference between transformers and CNNs?
context: Transformers rely mainly on attention for sequence modeling, while CNNs rely on convolution to learn local spatial patterns.
answer_hint: Transformers mainly use attention, while CNNs mainly use convolution.

question_variation: What is the difference between NLP and LLMs?
context: NLP is the broader field of language processing, while LLMs are one family of large-scale neural models used within NLP.
answer_hint: NLP is the broader field, while LLMs are a specific class of large neural models used in NLP.
